question_title: Will the U.S. Senate hold a floor vote on the Digital Asset Market CLARITY Act before July 24, 2026?
summary: The question resolves YES only if a recorded Senate floor roll-call vote (cloture, motion to proceed, or final passage) on the CLARITY Act occurs between July 11-23, 2026. As of July 10, no cloture motion was filed, no floor time was allocated, the merged text had not even been released (expected week of July 13), and the required ~60-vote bipartisan coalition did not exist amid three unresolved disputes. Status quo strongly favors NO, and legislative timelines slip heavily. The modest upside comes from a concrete 'week of July 20' floor-action target that overlaps the window and the pressure of an approaching August recess, which could prompt a forcing/messaging cloture vote (a failed vote would still count). Balancing these, I estimate ~12%.

factors.description: Bill placed on calendar but no cloture motion filed and no floor time allocated as of July 10 — status quo for calendared bills is prolonged inaction

Merged Banking+Agriculture text only expected to drop the week of July 13; 60-vote bipartisan coalition not yet formed with 3 unresolved disputes — prerequisites for a floor vote unmet with days to spare
Concrete industry/Lummis push and 'week of July 20' floor-action target overlaps the in-window days (July 20-23)
Legislative floor-action targets routinely slip; just-released merged text leaves ~1 week for Democrats to review before any vote

reasoning:
**Forecast: 12%**

The CLARITY Act question resolves YES only if the Senate holds a recorded floor roll-call vote (cloture, motion to proceed, or final passage) between July 11–23, 2026. My forecast is **12% (NO likely)**, driven by:

- **Against (strong):** As of July 10, no cloture motion filed, no floor time allocated, merged text not yet released (expected week of July 13), and no 60-vote bipartisan coalition — three disputes (ethics/Section 604, stablecoin yield, AML) still blocking Democratic votes.
- **For (modest):** A concrete "week of July 20" floor-action target overlaps the window, industry/Lummis pressure is real, and the looming August recess could prompt a forcing/messaging cloture vote (a failed vote still counts YES).

The prerequisites simply aren't in place with only days to spare, and legislative timing targets routinely slip.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between July 11, 2026 and July 23, 2026 (inclusive), the full U.S. Senate holds any recorded floor vote directly on the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act — meaning a roll-call vote on a motion to invoke cloture on, a motion to proceed to, or final passage of the bill (either H.R. 3633 as received from the House, or a Senate substitute/companion measure that Congress.gov identifies as the CLARITY Act market-structure bill). The vote need not succeed; a failed cloture vote or failed passage vote also counts. Resolution is based on the official actions history at https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/3633/all-actions (and, if a separate Senate bill number is used, that bill's Congress.gov actions page). It resolves NO if no such Senate floor roll-call vote is recorded in that window.
fine_print: Committee votes, markups, calendar placements, and procedural unanimous-consent agreements that are not recorded roll-call votes do not count. A voice vote explicitly recorded by the Senate as passing or rejecting the bill on the floor also counts as a floor vote for this question. If the primary Congress.gov page is unavailable, resolution may rely on the official Senate roll-call vote records or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

factors.description: Bill placed on calendar but no cloture motion filed, no floor time allocated as of July 10 — status quo for calendared bills is prolonged inaction
Merged text only expected to drop week of July 13; 60-vote bipartisan coalition not yet formed, 3 unresolved disputes — prerequisites for a floor vote not met with days to spare
Concrete industry/Lummis push and 'week of July 20' floor-action target overlaps the in-window days (July 20-23)
Legislative floor-action targets routinely slip; merged text just dropping leaves ~1 week for Democrats to review before a vote

input.anchor: Base rate for a calendared bill receiving a floor roll-call vote within a specific ~2-week window when no cloture is filed and no floor time allocated: low, ~15%
input.assessment: Question resolves YES only if a recorded Senate floor roll-call vote (cloture, motion to proceed, or final passage) occurs July 11-23. As of July 10, no cloture filed, no floor time allocated, merged text not even released, and the 60-vote coalition doesn't exist. Advocates target 'week of July 20' but that is aspirational and prerequisites are unmet. Legislative timelines slip heavily; a leader typically won't bring a filibuster-subject bill to the floor without votes lined up (though a messaging cloture vote is possible). Strong pull toward NO with modest upside from the concrete July-20 target within window.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo strongly favors NO. The 'week of July 20' target provides genuine upside but targets slip and window clips at July 23. Avoiding lazy 50%; committing near 12%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether Thune files a cloture/procedural vote as a forcing mechanism even without secured votes, and whether merged text drops on time enabling a July 20-23 vote.
input.tool_audit: web_search returned rich, directly relevant recent reporting (CoinDesk, Yahoo, TechTimes, cryptotimes) confirming state as of July 9-10. No failures.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward short-horizon legislative-timing question. Web search gave exactly the dated status needed. No friction; did not need subforecast decomposition given clear status-quo framing.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are grounded in the research trace with correct logit signs:

1. "No cloture filed, no floor time allocated" (-1.6): Trace confirms — "Calendar No. 423... no floor vote scheduled, no cloture motion filed." Correct NO-direction.
2. "Merged text drops week of July 13; 60-vote coalition not formed; 3 unresolved disputes" (-1.4): Trace confirms — "merged draft due next week," "three interlocking disputes still blocking the seven to nine Democratic votes," "still lacks bipartisan buy-in." Correct.
3. "Industry/Lummis push + 'week of July 20' floor target overlaps window" (+1.1): Trace confirms — "advocates eyeing floor action the week of July 20," "Senate Eyes July 20 Floor Vote." The Lummis-specific detail is a minor embellishment from the crypto.news snippet, but the core claim (July 20 floor target + industry push within the July 11-23 window) is well-supported. Correct YES-direction.
4. "Targets slip; ~1 week for Democrats to review" (-0.8): Reasonable inference grounded in the just-released-text timeline. Correct.

No hallucinations, no double-counting, no wrong-direction factors, no contradiction between assessment and factors.

Resolution criteria are provided in full, and the factors/assessment engage directly with the specific terms (recorded roll-call cloture/motion-to-proceed/final passage, the July 11-23 window, failed cloture still counting). Good alignment — no recovery needed.

No pre-publication event issue: published July 13, window opens July 11, but no vote or cloture filing has occurred yet — the forecast is genuinely forward-looking.

Anchor (~15%) vs final (12%): The final sits slightly BELOW the anchor because the conditions here are worse than a generic calendared bill (merged text not even released, coalition nonexistent). A modest, evidence-supported departure — no concern.

Worldview consistency: Two fresh research entries (u-s-senate-clarity-act... and senate-schedule-june-2026...) from June 18 fully corroborate the factors — Calendar No. 423, no floor vote scheduled, no companion bill, 60-vote filibuster threshold, Thune majority leader. No contradictions.

Counterargument is strong and genuine: it constructs a coherent alternative story (messaging cloture as a forcing mechanism, recess-deadline pressure, the July 20-23 tail inside the window, failed cloture still resolving YES) rather than restating the conclusion. Self-confidence of 0.75 is well-calibrated. Tool audit ("no failures") matches ground truth (6 calls, 0 errors).

Independent probability check: The dominant reality — no cloture filed, no floor time, merged text unreleased, no 60-vote coalition, with only ~9 legislative days after text drops — strongly pulls toward NO, with modest upside from the concrete in-window July 20 target and the hard recess deadline creating incentive for a forcing/messaging vote. A 10-18% estimate is well-justified; 12% sits squarely in that range and is consistent with the factor sum (~9%) plus a small nudge for the in-window target. I agree with the estimate.
